title: The Yoga Vasistha — The Complete Instruction
subtitle: The story of Vasistha instructing Rāma — guru-disciple transmission as the initiatic model; dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi-vāda as creation through consciousness; the 7 stages of wisdom (sapta bhūmika) as the ascent through the tattvas

summary.one_line: The Yoga Vāsiṣṭha analyzed as the most comprehensive Indian manual of spiritual instruction: the guru-disciple dialogue as initiatic transmission, creation as consciousness (dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi-vāda), the ...
summary.scope: Covers the Vairāgya (dispassion), Mumukṣu (seeker), Utpatti (creation), Sthiti (sustenance), Upāśama (calming), and Nirvāṇa (liberation) Prakaraṇas; the sapta bhūmikas; the theory of consciousness as the sole reality; the stories as didactic upāya; the doctrine of jīvanmukti.

body.text: [See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Vairāgya Prakaraṇa. The entire text is structured as a dialogue between the sage Vāsiṣṭha and Prince Rāma, who is afflicted with existential despair (vairāgya) at the suffering of the world. Rāma asks: 'What is this world? Who am I? What is bondage? How can I be free?' Vāsiṣṭha answers not with doctrine but with stories — each story is an upāya, a skillful means tailored to Rāma's specific question. The guru-disciple relationship is the initiatic model: the guru does not give information but imparts transformation. Vāsiṣṭha is the daimon speaking through the human teacher — the transmission is from consciousness to consciousness, not from person to person. The dialogue is the internal dialogue of the soul with its own higher self.]

[See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Utpatti Prakaraṇa. The theory of dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi-vāda: creation is nothing but perception — the world is not an objective reality independent of consciousness but is seen (dṛṣṭi) and thereby created (sṛṣṭi). 'The world is a long dream from which one must awaken. There is no world apart from the mind; the mind alone creates the world by its power of conception (kalpanā).' This is stronger than Śaṅkara's vivarta-vāda — it denies even the appearance of objectivity. Compare to Tantraloka's pratibimba-vāda: the world is the reflection of consciousness in itself. Both agree that the world has no independent existence. The difference is emphasis: Vasistha stresses the subjective creation (dṛṣṭi creates sṛṣṭi), Abhinavagupta stresses the reflective nature of consciousness (vimarśa projects ābhāsa). The daimon is the original perceiver — when we see as the daimon sees, we see the world as it truly is: consciousness appearing as form.]

[See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Nirvāṇa Prakaraṇa. The seven stages of wisdom (sapta bhūmika) are: (1) Īpsā (desire for liberation), (2) Vicāraṇa (inquiry), (3) Tanumānasa (subtle-mindedness), (4) Sattvāpatti (attainment of purity), (5) Asaṅga (non-attachment), (6) Padārtha-bhāvanā (non-perception of objects), (7) Turyagā (absorption in the fourth state). These seven stages are the ascent through the tattvas: from the gross (īpsā = earth) to the supremely subtle (turyagā = śiva-tattva). Stage 4 (sattvāpatti) is the 'awakening of the daimon-consciousness' — the first direct glimpse of the self as separate from the mind. Stage 7 (turyagā) is the unus-ambo realized: the soul established in the fourth state beyond waking, dream, and sleep, identical with the absolute. Compare to the seven stages of alchemical transformation in the Emerald Tablet.]

[See the embedded narratives throughout the Yoga Vāsiṣṭha: the story of Bhīma and the demon (Utpatti), the story of Līlā and her husband (Sthiti), the story of the King and the philosopher (Upāśama), the story of the rock that became a sage (Nirvāṇa). Each story is an upāya (skillful means) — a therapeutic narrative designed to produce a specific transformation in the hearer. 'The stories of the Vāsiṣṭha are not fables; they are mirrors held up to the mind so that it may recognize its own distorted reflections.' This is the Tantric principle of upāya: different paths for different souls. The daimon communicates through stories — not through abstract doctrine but through narratives that carry the transformative power directly. The story is the vehicle of the daimon's teaching, bypassing the intellect and speaking directly to the intuitive consciousness.]

[See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Utpatti Prakaraṇa. The mind (citta) is the sole creator of the world — not as a subjective illusion but as the actual substance of all experience. 'The mind alone is the universe. When the mind is active, the world is active; when the mind is still, the world ceases to exist.' This is the doctrine of the ekam eva advitīya (the one without a second) applied to psychology: the world is not Brahman directly but the mind's reflection of Brahman. The mind is the daimon's instrument — the power of consciousness to project worlds. When the mind is purified, it reflects the daimon directly without distortion. When the mind is agitated, it projects the world of saṃsāra. The practice of Yoga Vāsiṣṭha is the systematic purification of the mind so that it becomes the clear mirror of the daimon.]

[See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Nirvāṇa Prakaraṇa. The jīvanmukta (liberated while living) is described in great detail: one who is free from desires, whose mind is like the sky — clear, empty, and unperturbed; who acts without attachment; for whom the body continues like a lamp that flickers after the oil is gone. 'The jīvanmukta, though living in the body, is always established in the supreme Self. He sees the world as a dream within a dream. His actions are without doership, his thoughts without thinker.' This is the Completed Work — the Great Work realized. The daimon and the soul are united in life, not merely after death. The distinction between the human and the divine is erased — the jīvanmukta is the daimon walking the earth. The body is the temple of the completed work.]

[See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Upāśama Prakaraṇa. Vāsiṣṭha teaches Rāma the method of prāṇa-samrodha (restraint of the vital breath) as a direct means to still the mind. 'As long as there is the movement of prāṇa, there is the movement of the mind. When prāṇa is stilled, the mind is stilled. The mind and prāṇa are two branches of the same tree. When one is grasped, the other is caught.' This is the exact same doctrine as the Layayoga prāṇāyāma system and as Ajahn Lee's breath meditation. The breath is the handle of the mind — by mastering the breath, one masters the mind. By mastering the mind, one reveals the daimon. This is the universal teaching that runs through all traditions: the breath is the bridge between the human and the divine.]

[See Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, Nirvāṇa Prakaraṇa. The highest teaching: ajāti-vāda (non-origination) — the world was never created, never exists, and never ceases to exist. 'There is no creation, no destruction, no bondage, no liberation, no seeker, no one who has attained. The supreme Reality alone exists, without beginning, without end, without change.' This is the 'neti, neti' (not thus, not thus) of the Upaniṣads pushed to its ultimate conclusion. Even the distinction between the daimon and the soul is a superimposition. The unus-ambo is not a union of two things but the recognition that there were never two things to begin with. The daimon was never separate — the realization of the daimon is the realization that you have always been the daimon. This is the final word of the Yoga Vāsiṣṭha: the silence beyond all teaching.]

gaps: Detailed treatment of all six Prakaraṇas needed; complete passage extraction from the massive Mitra translation
Detailed analysis of the specific stories and their didactic function needed

issues.title: Passages need direct quotation from primary text
issues.description: Currently referencing source synthesis. The Mitra translation (158K lines) is the primary source — passages need precise extraction with volume/chapter/verse references.

issues.title: Missing analysis of the story of Līlā and the theory of parallel universes
issues.description: The Līlā story in the Sthiti Prakaraṇa contains a sophisticated doctrine of multiple realities and the power of consciousness to create worlds — needs integration with the daimon cosmology.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. 8 passages covering guru-disciple transmission, dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi-vāda, sapta bhūmika, stories as upāya, mind as creator, jīvanmukti, prāṇa-samrodha, and ajāti-vāda.
